one_line_takeaway: By injecting a backdoor only into VLA modules that barely change during fine-tuning, INFUSE makes the attack survive clean downstream adaptation, keeping 79-95% attack success where prior methods drop to 30-45%.
key_finding: After user-side clean fine-tuning, INFUSE retains high attack success while baselines collapse. On OpenVLA-7B / LIBERO it reaches 95.3% average ASR vs BadVLA 31.7%, Adversarial-VLA 27.4%, BadNet 7.8% (Table 1); on pi0.5 / LIBERO 85.9% vs BadVLA 45.2% (Table 2); on SpatialVLA-4B / SimplerEnv WidowX 91.7% vs BadVLA 39.4% (Table 3); and on the real Franka robot 79.8% vs BadVLA 36.6% (Table 4), all while preserving clean no-trigger success rates comparable to the normal model (e.g. real-world SR(w/o) 28.3% vs 29.3%). An ablation (Table 5) confirms the mechanism: injecting into insensitive modules gives 95.3% ASR, versus 42.2% for full-model injection and only 10.8% for sensitive-module injection. INFUSE also resists three defenses (Table 6): under JPEG compression (down to q=20%), Gaussian noise (up to epsilon=0.08), and delta-W parameter auditing (up to 20% modules reset), ASR stays at or above 87% while clean SR stays ~91-95%.
core_contribution: The paper introduces INFUSE (INjection into Fine-tUne-inSensitive modulEs), described as the first backdoor attack on pre-trained base Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models that remains effective after arbitrary user-side clean fine-tuning. The key insight, and the paper's own contribution, is that certain VLA modules (vision backbone, vision projector, LLM backbone) undergo 100-1000x smaller parameter updates during downstream adaptation than others (action head, proprio projector). INFUSE identifies these "fine-tune-insensitive" modules via a stability analysis and injects the backdoor exclusively into them while freezing the rest, so the malicious behavior survives clean fine-tuning. This is contrasted with prior work such as BadVLA [50] and Adversarial-VLA [35], which the authors show collapse after clean fine-tuning because they assume the attacker can influence user-side training — these are cited baselines, not this paper's method.
threat_model: Attacker fully controls a pre-trained VLA foundation model's parameters before distribution but has NO access to the victim's downstream fine-tuning data and cannot modify the model after distribution. The victim later fine-tunes the received base model on their own clean, trigger-free data. The attacker's goal is a backdoor that (1) preserves benign behavior on clean inputs, (2) triggers a fixed malicious action when a physical object trigger (e.g., a blue mug) is present, and (3) persists through the victim's clean fine-tuning. This is a supply-chain / pre-distribution poisoning threat model, contrasted with prior work (BadVLA) that unrealistically assumed attacker influence over user-side fine-tuning.
limitations: The authors state INFUSE currently relies on static triggers and fixed target behaviors, and suggest future work on dynamic or instruction-aware triggers and other architectures. Beyond what the paper admits: real-world clean success rates are intrinsically low for some tasks (e.g. normal-model SR of 16-29% on several SimplerEnv/real tasks), so ASR is measured against a weak base policy. There is also a numerical inconsistency between the abstract (which reports 91.0% simulation ASR) and the intro/conclusion/tables (95.3% LIBERO, 91.7% SimplerEnv); the tables are the authoritative results. The delta-W auditing defense is only tested up to 20% of modules, and no adaptive defense specifically targeting static/insensitive modules is evaluated.

datasets_used: Real-world Franka Research 3 tabletop tasks (Knock Over, Cover with Towel, Pick into Box)
quantitative_results: OpenVLA-7B/LIBERO avg ASR: INFUSE 95.3% vs BadVLA 31.7%, Adversarial-VLA 27.4%, BadNet 7.8% (Table 1)
pi0.5/LIBERO avg ASR: INFUSE 85.9% vs BadVLA 45.2% (Table 2)
SpatialVLA-4B/SimplerEnv WidowX avg ASR: INFUSE 91.7% vs BadVLA 39.4% (Table 3)
Real-world Franka avg ASR: INFUSE 79.8% vs BadVLA 36.6%; clean SR(w/o) 28.3% vs normal 29.3% (Table 4)

Fine-tune-insensitive modules update 100-1000x less than sensitive ones (intro); OpenVLA-OFT sensitivity scores ~0.01 LLM backbone / 0.33 vision backbone / 0.17 vision projector vs 0.84 action head / 0.97 proprio projector (Fig 3)
Ablation (Table 5): insensitive-module injection 95.3% avg ASR vs full-model 42.2% vs sensitive-module 10.8%
Defense robustness (Table 6): ASR stays >=87% under JPEG q=20%, Gaussian noise epsilon=0.08, and 20% delta-W module auditing (ASR 90.4%), with clean SR ~91-95%
Clean-task performance preserved: 95.0% vs standard model 96.4% (abstract/intro)
